content.text: Every GenAI architecture decision carries an implicit option: the option to reverse course. The cost of exercising that option varies by orders of magnitude depending on what the decision touches. Embedding that reversibility analysis into every architectural choice — before committing resources — is the discipline that separates teams that evolve cleanly from teams that rewrite from scratch every eighteen months.

attrs.alt: Architecture decision reversibility spectrum: Reversible (two-way door), Partially Reversible, and Irreversible (one-way door) categories with GenAI examples and process requirements for each

content.text: The Two-Door Mental Model

Amazon's two-door framework translates directly to GenAI architecture: some decisions are reversible (two-way doors) and some are not (one-way doors). The error most teams make is treating all decisions as two-way doors and moving fast everywhere — then discovering that a core embedding model choice from six months ago now locks them into a vector index of 40 million embeddings that would take three weeks to rebuild.

The discipline is not to slow down on reversible decisions. It is to accurately identify which decisions are irreversible and slow down  {'type': 'text', 'text': 'only', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]}  on those.
For GenAI systems specifically, three forces make irreversibility more common than in conventional software:
Data artifacts accumulate.
A vector index, a fine-tune dataset, or an eval benchmark is not just configuration — it encodes months of curation effort that cannot be swapped without rebuilding the artifact.
Model output distributions shift non-linearly.
Changing an embedding model by a minor version can invalidate all retrieval rankings. The data contract between components is statistical, not typed.
Regulatory and compliance constraints compound.

A data residency decision (EU vs US) or a model hosting choice (cloud vs on-prem) immediately constrains every downstream component choice and is rarely reversible without a full infrastructure migration.
Reversibility Classification for GenAI Decisions
Before starting any significant technical thread, classify it using this rubric:
Dimension
Question to ask
High reversibility
Low reversibility
Data artifact impact
Does this decision produce or depend on a large, expensive-to-rebuild artifact?
No durable artifact (config, param)
Embedding index, fine-tune dataset, trace schema
Integration surface
How many external systems depend on the new interface?
Internal service boundary only
External APIs, data partners, compliance systems
Rollback cost
What is the estimated time and effort to reverse?
< 1 day, automated rollback
> 2 sprints, requires data migration
Eval reference change
Does this invalidate the existing benchmark?
Benchmark still valid
Must relabel eval set, losing historical comparisons
Regulatory lock-in
Does this commit to a legal or compliance regime?
No new regulatory scope
New jurisdiction, data category, or model tier
Scoring and Routing

Score each dimension 1 (high reversibility) to 3 (low reversibility). Total ≤ 5 → fast-track decision. Total 6–10 → spike + ADR required. Total ≥ 11 → multi-party review and explicit option modeling before committing.
Preserving Options: Design Patterns
Beyond classification, there are concrete design patterns that keep options open longer — deferring irreversible commitments until more information is available.
Pattern 1: Provider Abstraction Layer

The most common irreversible decision in GenAI is choosing a model provider and calling their SDK directly throughout the codebase. When the provider raises prices, hits an outage, or releases an inferior model update, migration requires touching every call site.

from abc import ABC, abstractmethod
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Any

@dataclass
class LLMRequest:
    prompt: str
    model: str        # logical name: "chat-fast", "chat-quality", "embedder"
    temperature: float = 0.0
    max_tokens: int = 1024

@dataclass
class LLMResponse:
    text: str
    input_tokens: int
    output_tokens: int
    latency_ms: float

class LLMAdapter(ABC):
    """Abstract boundary. Implementations are swappable without touching callers."""
    @abstractmethod
    def complete(self, req: LLMRequest) -> LLMResponse: ...

class AnthropicAdapter(LLMAdapter):
    def __init__(self, model_map: dict[str, str]):
        import anthropic
        self._client = anthropic.Anthropic()
        self._map = model_map  # logical → physical model id

    def complete(self, req: LLMRequest) -> LLMResponse:
        import time
        physical = self._map.get(req.model, req.model)
        t0 = time.monotonic()
        resp = self._client.messages.create(
            model=physical,
            max_tokens=req.max_tokens,
            temperature=req.temperature,
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": req.prompt}],
        )
        return LLMResponse(
            text=resp.content[0].text,
            input_tokens=resp.usage.input_tokens,
            output_tokens=resp.usage.output_tokens,
            latency_ms=(time.monotonic() - t0) * 1000,
        )

# All callers use LLMAdapter — switching providers requires
# only a new adapter implementation, not call-site changes.
Pattern 2: Embedding Index Versioning

Once a vector index is built with a specific embedding model, changing the model invalidates the entire index. Versioning the index alongside the model prevents a silent mismatch from degrading retrieval quality:
Pattern 3: ADR with Explicit Reversal Criteria

For irreversible decisions, write an Architecture Decision Record that specifies not only why you chose X over Y, but also the conditions under which you would reverse the decision. This converts a one-way door into a one-way door with a known emergency exit:

# ADR-014: Embedding Model — text-embedding-3-large

## Status
Accepted — 2025-03-12

## Context
We need a stable embedding model for the production knowledge base (1.2M chunks).
Rebuilding the index takes ~18 hours of compute. The model choice is therefore
partially irreversible until we schedule a maintenance window.

## Decision
Use `text-embedding-3-large` (3072-dim, truncated to 1536 for cost).

## Alternatives Considered
- `text-embedding-3-small`: 23% lower NDCG@10 on our internal eval set
- Cohere embed-english-v3.0: Adds vendor dependency; no cost advantage at our scale

## Consequences
- Index rebuild required if this model is deprecated or if we switch providers.
- All retrieval evals tied to this model's distance geometry.

## Reversal Criteria  ← explicit option
Reverse this decision if ANY of:
1. Provider deprecates the model with < 90 days notice
2. NDCG@10 on eval set drops > 5% vs a challenger embedding model in A/B test
3. Cost per 1M tokens increases > 2x current pricing
4. A new model achieves ≥ 8% NDCG@10 improvement in head-to-head eval

## Reversal Cost Estimate
Index rebuild: ~18 hours compute, ~$240 at current rates.
Eval relabeling: ~40 human-review hours (20% sample requiring human adjudication).
Total migration window: 1 sprint.
Option Value in Architecture

Real options theory — from finance — applies directly to GenAI architecture: keeping a decision open has value when uncertainty is high and new information will reduce that uncertainty over time. The option has a carrying cost (the overhead of maintaining flexibility) and an exercise price (the cost to change once you decide).
Applying this to GenAI architecture decisions:
Decision
Option to keep open
Carrying cost
When to exercise
Which fine-tuning provider to use
Maintain training pipeline that targets adapter format, not provider SDK
~2 days one-time abstraction cost
When provider pricing or quality diverges > 20%
On-prem vs cloud inference
Deploy containerized inference, parameterize endpoint URL
~3 days infra setup
At 18-month review if cloud costs exceed threshold
Structured output schema for evals
Use versioned schema registry, migrate readers not writers
Schema registry operational cost
When eval rubric fundamentally changes
Proprietary vs open-weight model
Keep prompt library provider-agnostic; maintain eval on both
~10% overhead in eval runs
When open-weight model closes quality gap to < 5%

The principle is that uncertainty about the right choice should make you  {'type': 'text', 'text': 'buy the option', 'marks': [{'type': 'italic'}]}  (preserve flexibility) rather than decide now. Once you have 90 days of production data, the uncertainty drops sharply and the option can be exercised or abandoned cheaply.
The Reversibility Review in Practice
Embed reversibility analysis into the team's existing decision processes rather than creating a separate review ceremony:
In Sprint Planning
Any technical story that touches a data artifact, provider integration, or eval schema should be tagged with its reversibility score at refinement time.
Stories with total score ≥ 11 need a spike first — the spike output is the ADR with reversal criteria, not working code.
Stories with score 6–10 get a brief async ADR (15-minute write-up) before implementation begins.
In Architecture Reviews
Add a fixed section to every architecture review document:

## Reversibility Analysis

| Decision | Score (1–15) | Routing | Reversal criteria |
|---|---|---|---|
| Switch RAG chunker to semantic splitter | 8 | spike+ADR | NDCG@10 drops > 3% or latency > 800ms p99 |
| Adopt Bedrock for inference | 12 | full-review | Pricing > 1.5x current; latency SLO breach |
| Add structured output guardrail layer | 5 | fast-track | Any functional regression in 72h canary |
In Incident Retrospectives

After any incident caused by a dependency on a specific model version, provider, or data schema, add a retro action item to reclassify affected decisions. If a "reversible" decision turned out to be hard to reverse, update the team's classification rubric.
Common Traps
"We can always change it later."

The implicit assumption is that the reversal cost is low. For GenAI systems with accumulating data artifacts, it almost never is. Require explicit reversal cost estimates before accepting this reasoning.
Confusing configuration with architecture.

Changing a temperature value is reversible in minutes. Changing the prompt structure that the entire downstream parser depends on is a data contract change. Both live in "config" but have very different reversibility profiles.
Over-abstracting to preserve options.

An abstraction layer for every dependency adds complexity that itself becomes hard to change. Apply the provider abstraction only where switching is genuinely plausible within the product's lifecycle.
ADRs without reversal criteria.
An ADR that only records what was decided and why provides no guidance on when to revisit. The reversal criteria are the most operationally valuable part of the document.
Not updating reversibility scores after major milestones.

A decision that was partially reversible at 10,000 users may be effectively irreversible at 10 million users because the data artifact has grown by 1000x. Re-score after each significant scale change.
Summary
Architecture decision reversibility is not a philosophical property — it is an operational cost that can be estimated, managed, and sometimes reduced through deliberate design. The workflow is:
Classify every significant decision on the reversibility spectrum before committing.
Score across five dimensions: data artifact impact, integration surface, rollback cost, eval reference change, and regulatory lock-in.
Route to the right process: fast-track (≤ 5), spike + ADR (6–10), or full review (≥ 11).
For irreversible decisions, write explicit reversal criteria in the ADR — the specific conditions under which the team will revisit the choice.
Apply the provider abstraction layer and index versioning patterns to convert one-way doors into partially reversible ones wherever the carrying cost is acceptable.
Re-score decisions when scale changes substantially or when an incident reveals hidden coupling.
Teams that internalize this discipline spend their architecture change budget on intentional improvements rather than forced migrations.
